title: EU ePrivacy — Directive 2002/58/EC and Proposed ePrivacy Regulation
official_title: Directive 2002/58/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (ePrivacy Directive) and Proposal for a Regulation on Privacy and Electronic Communications (ePrivacy Regulation)
status: ePrivacy Directive in force; Regulation proposal stalled
overview: The ePrivacy Directive (2002/58/EC, amended 2009 by Directive 2009/136/EC) governs privacy in electronic communications — covering cookies, direct marketing, confidentiality of communications, and traffic/location data. It is a lex specialis to GDPR for electronic communications. The proposed ePrivacy Regulation (2017) would replace the Directive with a directly applicable regulation but has been stalled in negotiations for years. Member states have implemented the Directive differently, creating inconsistencies across the EU.

cookie_consent.requirement: Article 5(3): Storing or accessing information on a user's terminal equipment requires prior informed consent.
cookie_consent.exceptions: Strictly necessary cookies (no consent required): session cookies, security cookies, load-balancing cookies, authentication cookies.
cookie_consent.implementation: Member states implement differently — some require explicit click, others allow implied consent through browser settings. Enforcement varies significantly.
cookie_consent.consent_banner_requirements: Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous under GDPR (applicable to cookies). Pre-ticked boxes prohibited. Withdrawing consent must be as easy as giving it.

direct_marketing.email: Opt-in required for electronic marketing to individuals. Soft opt-in exception: existing customer relationship, similar products/services, opt-out offered.
direct_marketing.calls: Opt-out mechanism required for live calls. Automated calls require opt-in.
direct_marketing.b2b: Member state discretion — many allow opt-out model for B2B marketing.

traffic_and_location_data.traffic_data: Must be erased or anonymised when no longer needed for transmission. Billing and interconnect exceptions.
traffic_and_location_data.location_data: More restrictive — consent required or anonymisation. Users must be informed of type and purpose before consent.
traffic_and_location_data.retention: Data retention laws vary by member state under national security exception.

current_law_eprivacy_directive.confidentiality: Prohibition on interception and surveillance of communications without consent or legal authority.

proposed_eprivacy_regulation.status: Proposed by Commission in January 2017. Still in negotiation as of 2026. No agreed text.
key_changes: Expanded scope to cover OTT services (WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage) — 'internet-based communications'
Cookie consent: browser-level consent settings could replace per-website banners
Harmonised enforcement — DPAs enforce alongside national telecom regulators
Penalties aligned with GDPR: up to €20M or 4% of global turnover
Abolish 'cookie walls' — cannot condition access to service on consent to non-necessary cookies
proposed_eprivacy_regulation.obstacles: Significant disagreement on: scope of OTT services, cookie consent mechanisms, business vs individual privacy, member state flexibility. Expected to remain stalled.

enforcement_landscape.national_variation: Significant enforcement variation across EU. Germany, France, Netherlands most active on cookie enforcement.
enforcement_landscape.dpa_enforcement: GDPR DPAs enforce cookie consent violations in many member states.
enforcement_landscape.telecom_regulators: Separate telecom regulators enforce communications confidentiality provisions.
enforcement_landscape.notable_cases: Google (€150M France, 2022), Facebook/Meta (€60M France, 2022), Amazon (€35M France, 2021) — all for cookie consent failures.

penalty_structure.current_directive: Member state rules — widely varying. Fines typically €100,000–€20M depending on member state.
penalty_structure.proposed_regulation: Up to €20M or 4% of global turnover (aligned with GDPR Tier 2).

cross_references.relevance: ePrivacy is lex specialis to GDPR for electronic communications. Cookie consent governed by both: ePrivacy Directive requires consent (mechanism); GDPR defines what valid consent means. For cookies, both laws apply simultaneously.

AI-powered advertising targeting and profiling using cookies/tracking must comply with ePrivacy consent requirements and GDPR. AI systems processing communications data face ePrivacy confidentiality requirements.
Data Act governs IoT/connected device data. ePrivacy governs electronic communications. Some IoT devices with communication functions overlap between both frameworks.
